{{Infobox shrine | name = Ōi Shrine (大井神社) | image = [[File:Oi Jinja Mito Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]] Iitomi-chō 3475 | coordinates = 36°25′28″N 140°24′52″E | deity = {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=}} | shrine_ranking = {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (minor), {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=}}; former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=}} | established = Tradition: Reign of Emperor Sujin (10th century BC) | main_hall_style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | annual_festival = April 10 | map = Japan Ibaraki }} {{GeoGroup}} '''Ōi Shrine''' (Ōi-jinja, 大井神社) is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * On-site information boards * 『日本歴史地名体系 茨城県の地名』 (Heibonsha, 1982), entry “Mito Ōi Shrine” == See also == * {{ill|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|de|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|仲国造|zh|仲国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|de|Ōi-Schrein (Kasama)|ja|大井神社 (笠間市)|zh|大井神社 (笠间市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kenou/jinja/01042.html Ōi Shrine] — Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Office {{Navbox-Shinto}} {{Navbox-IbarakiShrines}} [[Category:Translated pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Mito, Ibaraki]] [[ja:大井神社 (水戸市)]] [[de:Ōi-Schrein (Mito)]] [[zh:大井神社 (水户市)]] [[sn:Ōi Shrine (Mito)]] [[en:Ōi Shrine (Mito)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:水戸市の建築物]] [[Category:水戸市の歴史]]

{{Infobox shrine | name = Ōi Shrine (大井神社) | image = [[File:Oi Jinja Mito Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]] Iitomi-chō 3475 | coordinates = 36°25′28″N 140°24′52″E | deity = {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=}} | shrine_ranking = {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (minor), {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=}}; former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=}} | established = Tradition: Reign of Emperor Sujin (10th century BC) | main_hall_style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | annual_festival = April 10 | map = Japan Ibaraki }} {{GeoGroup}} '''Ōi Shrine''' (Ōi-jinja, 大井神社) is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * On-site information boards * 『日本歴史地名体系 茨城県の地名』 (Heibonsha, 1982), entry “Mito Ōi Shrine” == See also == * {{ill|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|de|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|仲国造|zh|仲国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|de|Ōi-Schrein (Kasama)|ja|大井神社 (笠間市)|zh|大井神社 (笠间市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kenou/jinja/01042.html Ōi Shrine] — Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Office {{Navbox-Shinto}} {{Navbox-IbarakiShrines}} [[Category:Translated pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Mito, Ibaraki]] [[ja:大井神社 (水戸市)]] [[de:Ōi-Schrein (Mito)]] [[zh:大井神社 (水户市)]] [[sn:Ōi Shrine (Mito)]] [[en:Ōi Shrine (Mito)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Mito, Ibaraki]] [[Category:History of Mito, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]]

{{Infobox shrine | name = Ōi Shrine (大井神社) | image = [[File:Oi Jinja Mito Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]] Iitomi-chō 3475 | coordinates = 36°25′28″N 140°24′52″E | deity = {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=}} | shrine_ranking = {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (minor), {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=}}; former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=}} | established = Tradition: Reign of Emperor Sujin (10th century BC) | main_hall_style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | annual_festival = April 10 | map = Japan Ibaraki }} {{GeoGroup}} '''Ōi Shrine''' (Ōi-jinja, 大井神社) is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * On-site information boards * 『日本歴史地名体系 茨城県の地名』 (Heibonsha, 1982), entry “Mito Ōi Shrine” == See also == * {{ill|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|de|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|仲国造|zh|仲国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|de|Ōi-Schrein (Kasama)|ja|大井神社 (笠間市)|zh|大井神社 (笠间市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kenou/jinja/01042.html Ōi Shrine] — Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Office [[zh:大井神社 (水户市)]] [[sn:Ōi Shrine (Mito)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Mito, Ibaraki]] [[Category:History of Mito, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Oi-jinja (Mito)}} [[Category:pages without valid translation templates or en interwikis]]

{{Infobox shrine | name = Ōi Shrine (大井神社) | image = [[File:Oi Jinja Mito Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]] Iitomi-chō 3475 | coordinates = 36°25′28″N 140°24′52″E | deity = {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | shrine_ranking = {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} (minor), {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}; former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | established = Tradition: Reign of Emperor Sujin (10th century BC) | main_hall_style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | annual_festival = April 10 | map = Japan Ibaraki }} {{GeoGroup}} '''Ōi Shrine''' (Ōi-jinja, 大井神社) is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * On-site information boards * 『日本歴史地名体系 茨城県の地名』 (Heibonsha, 1982), entry “Mito Ōi Shrine” == See also == * {{ill|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|de|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|仲国造|zh|仲国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|de|Ōi-Schrein (Kasama)|ja|大井神社 (笠間市)|zh|大井神社 (笠间市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kenou/jinja/01042.html Ōi Shrine] — Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Office {{Navbox-Shinto}} {{Navbox-IbarakiShrines}} [[Category:Translated pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Mito, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Pages without valid translation templates or en interwikis but with jawiki links]][[ja:大井神社 (水戸市)]] [[de:Ōi-Schrein (Mito)]] [[zh:大井神社 (水户市)]] [[sn:Ōi Shrine (Mito)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Mito, Ibaraki]] [[Category:History of Mito, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Oi-jinja (Mito)}} [[Category:pages without valid translation templates or en interwikis]]

{{Infobox shrine | name = Ōi Shrine (大井神社) | image = [[File:Oi Jinja Mito Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]] Iitomi-chō 3475 | coordinates = 36°25′28″N 140°24′52″E | deity = {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | shrine_ranking = {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} (minor), {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}; former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | established = Tradition: Reign of Emperor Sujin (10th century BC) | main_hall_style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | annual_festival = April 10 | map = Japan Ibaraki }} {{GeoGroup}} '''Ōi Shrine''' (Ōi-jinja, 大井神社) is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * On-site information boards * 『日本歴史地名体系 茨城県の地名』 (Heibonsha, 1982), entry “Mito Ōi Shrine” == See also == * {{ill|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|de|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|仲国造|zh|仲国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|de|Ōi-Schrein (Kasama)|ja|大井神社 (笠間市)|zh|大井神社 (笠间市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kenou/jinja/01042.html Ōi Shrine] — Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Office [[zh:大井神社 (水户市)]] [[sn:Ōi Shrine (Mito)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Mito, Ibaraki]] [[Category:History of Mito, Ibaraki]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Oi-jinja (Mito)}} [[Category:pages without valid translation templates or en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages without valid {{Infobox shrine | name = Ōi Shrine (大井神社) | image = [[File:Oi Jinja Mito Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]] Iitomi-chō 3475 | coordinates = 36°25′28″N 140°24′52″E | deity = {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | shrine_ranking = {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} (minor), {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}; former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | established = Tradition: Reign of Emperor Sujin (10th century BC) | main_hall_style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | annual_festival = April 10 | map = Japan Ibaraki }} {{GeoGroup}} '''Ōi Shrine''' (Ōi-jinja, 大井神社) is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * On-site information boards * 『日本歴史地名体系 茨城県の地名』 (Heibonsha, 1982), entry “Mito Ōi Shrine” == See also == * {{ill|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|de|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|仲国造|zh|仲国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|de|Ōi-Schrein (Kasama)|ja|大井神社 (笠間市)|zh|大井神社 (笠间市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kenou/jinja/01042.html Ōi Shrine] — Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Office In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification Haiden (worship hall) | location = [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]] Iitomi-chō 3475 | coordinates = 36°25′28″N 140°24′52″E | deity = {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | shrine_ranking = {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} (minor), {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}; former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | established = Tradition: Reign of Emperor Sujin (10th century BC) | main_hall_style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | annual_festival = April 10 | map = Japan Ibaraki }} {{GeoGroup}} '''Ōi Shrine''' (Ōi-jinja, 大井神社) is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|zh|村社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * On-site information boards * 『日本歴史地名体系 茨城県の地名』 (Heibonsha, 1982), entry “Mito Ōi Shrine” == See also == * {{ill|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|de|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|仲国造|zh|仲国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|de|Ōi-Schrein (Kasama)|ja|大井神社 (笠間市)|zh|大井神社 (笠间市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kenou/jinja/01042.html Ōi Shrine] — Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Office {{Infobox shrine | name = Ōi Shrine (大井神社) | image = [[File:Oi Jinja Mito Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]] Iitomi-chō 3475 | coordinates = 36°25′28″N 140°24′52″E | deity = {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | shrine_ranking = {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} (minor), {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}; former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|zh|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | established = Tradition: Reign of Emperor Sujin (10th century BC) | main_hall_style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | annual_festival = April 10 | map = Japan Ibaraki }} {{GeoGroup}} '''Ōi Shrine''' (Ōi-jinja, 大井神社) is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|zh|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * On-site information boards * 『日本歴史地名体系 茨城県の地名』 (Heibonsha, 1982), entry “Mito Ōi Shrine” == See also == * {{ill|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|de|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|仲国造|zh|仲国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|de|Ōi-Schrein (Kasama)|ja|大井神社 (笠間市)|zh|大井神社 (笠间市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kenou/jinja/01042.html Ōi Shrine] — Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Office [[zh:大井神社 (水户市)]] [[sn:Ōi Shrine (Mito)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Mito, Ibaraki]] [[Category:History of {{Infobox shrine | name = Ōi Shrine (大井神社) | image = [[File:Oi Jinja Mito Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]] Iitomi-chō 3475 | coordinates = 36°25′28″N 140°24′52″E | deity = {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | shrine_ranking = {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor), {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}; former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|zh|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | established = Tradition: Reign of Emperor Sujin (10th century BC) | main_hall_style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | annual_festival = April 10 | map = Japan Ibaraki }} {{GeoGroup}} '''Ōi Shrine''' (Ōi-jinja, 大井神社) is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|zh|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * On-site information boards * 『日本歴史地名体系 茨城県の地名』 (Heibonsha, 1982), entry “Mito Ōi Shrine” == See also == * (笠间市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kenou/jinja/01042.html Ōi Shrine] — Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Office The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|1=Engishiki_Jinmyōchō#Ronsha|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho|lt=Ronsha}}}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|zh|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11432217]] Wikidata name: Oi-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōi Shrine (大井神社) | image = [[File:Oi Jinja Mito Haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) | location = [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]] Iitomi-chō 3475 | coordinates = 36°25′28″N 140°24′52″E | deity = {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | shrine_ranking = {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor), {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|1=Engishiki_Jinmyōchō#Ronsha|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho|lt=Ronsha}}}}; former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|zh|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | established = Tradition: Reign of Emperor Sujin (10th century BC) | main_hall_style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | annual_festival = April 10 | map = Japan Ibaraki }} {{GeoGroup}} '''Ōi Shrine''' (Ōi-jinja, 大井神社) is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|1=Engishiki_Jinmyōchō#Ronsha|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho|lt=Ronsha}}}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|zh|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. == Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko. '''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine. === About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign『国造本紀』仲国造条。. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。. Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines. == History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill. In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto. === Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha* [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*. In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system. == Shrine facilities == File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine == Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)Festivals per on-site board. * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31) == Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Mito, Ibaraki '''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * On-site information boards * 『日本歴史地名体系 茨城県の地名』 (Heibonsha, 1982), entry “Mito Ōi Shrine” == See also == * {{ill|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|de|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|仲国造|zh|仲国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|de|Ōi-Schrein (Kasama)|ja|大井神社 (笠間市)|zh|大井神社 (笠间市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kenou/jinja/01042.html Ōi Shrine] — Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Office '''Ōi Shrine''' (Ōi-jinja, 大井神社) is a Shinto shrine in [[:en:Mito, Ibaraki|Mito, Ibaraki]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sanctum|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|1=Engishiki_Jinmyōchō#Ronsha|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho|lt=Ronsha}}}}, with its modern shrine ranking as a former {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|zh|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}.
== Worshipped deities == {{see also|{{ill|Saijin|de|Saijin|ja|祭神|zh|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|qq=}}}} '''Primary deity''' * {{ill|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|de|Takekashima-no-Mikoto|ja|建借馬命|zh|建借马命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekashima-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} — the first head of the Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko.
'''Secondary deity''' * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|de|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫命|zh|木花开耶姬命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} — enshrined by amalgamation of a subordinate shrine.
=== About the primary deity === According to the *Kuni-no-Miyatsuko Honki* (volume 10 of the *Sendai Kuji Hongi*), Takekashima-no-Mikoto shares ancestry with the Iyo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko and was appointed the first Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko during Emperor Seimu’s reign<ref>『国造本紀』仲国造条。</ref>. The *Hitachi-no-Kuni Fudoki* records him (as “Takekashima-no-Mikoto”) subduing eastern rebels under Emperor Sujin<ref>『常陸国風土記』行方郡条。</ref>.
Remains linked to him include the Atago-yama Kofun (Atagoyama tumulus) in Atago-chō, Mito (traditionally his grave)<ref>[http://www.edu.pref.ibaraki.jp/… 愛宕山古墳] (Ibaraki Prefectural Board of Education).</ref><ref name="explain"/>. The shrine’s name “Ōi” and the place name “Iitomi” are also said to derive from his descendant Ōfu-no-Omi, descended from Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto<ref name="explain"/>.
In *Jinmyōchō Kōshō*, degree of freedom names him as Kamu-Yai-Mimi-no-Mikoto. Some traditions link “Kashima” in his name to enshrinement by the Kasuga (Kashima) and Katori shrines.
== History == === Origins === Shrine tradition holds that on Emperor Sujin’s order, Prince Toyokiiri-hiko-no-Mikoto brought Takekashima-no-Mikoto here and enshrined Amaterasu-Ōmikami on a nearby hill<ref name="explain"/>.
In the Nara period, the ujibe clan of Naka District worshipped Takekashima-no-Mikoto<ref name="explain"/>.
=== Overview === In the mid-Heian period, the *Engishiki Jinja-myochō* lists it as “Ōi Shrine of Naka District, Hitachi Province,” ranking it among the *Shikinaisha*<ref> [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/… 大井神社] (Ibaraki Shrine Office).</ref>. However, a decree of junior fifth rank from 845 AD likely refers to another Ōi Shrine in Suruga Province. The Ōi Shrine in Kasama is also a *Ronsya*.
In the late Muromachi period, the shrine burned; it was rebuilt in Eishō (1504–1521) but again damaged by fire in 1588. In 1668, Tokugawa Mitsukuni ordered repairs, naming it “Katori Daimyōjin,” but it reverted to Ōi Shrine during Kansei (1789–1801). The current haiden dates to Ansei (1854–1859). In the Meiji era it was designated a village shrine under the modern ranking system.
== Shrine facilities == <gallery> File:Oi Jinja Mito Honden.jpg|Main hall (honden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Ihaiden.jpg|Spirit tablet hall (ihaiden) File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha1.jpg|Ofubiten Tatsumi Shrine File:Oi Jinja Mito Massha2.jpg|Camellia Shrine </gallery>
== Festivals == * New Year’s Festival (January 1)<ref>Festivals per on-site board.</ref> * Great Gate-Opening Festival (January 7) * Setsubun Festival (February 3) * Annual Festival (April 10) * Rice-Planting Festival (July 14) * Niiname Festival (November 23) * Great Purification (December 31)
== Location and access == '''Location''' * {{ill|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|de|Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|ja|飯富町3475|zh|饭富町3475|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iitomi-chō 3475, Mito|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Mito, Ibaraki
'''Access''' * JR East Jōban Line from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] ** Bus: Ibaraki Kōtsū to “Ōi Shrine-mae” stop
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * On-site information boards * 『日本歴史地名体系 茨城県の地名』 (Heibonsha, 1982), entry “Mito Ōi Shrine”
== See also == * {{ill|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|de|Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|仲国造|zh|仲国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakakuni-no-Miyatsuko|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|de|Ōi-Schrein (Kasama)|ja|大井神社 (笠間市)|zh|大井神社 (笠间市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Kasama)|qq=}}
